coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are considered emerging nanomaterials for photothermal therapy (PTT) of cancer due to their good biocompatibility and rapid excretion. However, the optical absorbance of GQDs in shorter wavelengths (<1000 nm) limits their overall therapeutic efficacies as photothermal agent in the second near infrared window (1000–1700 nm, NIR-II). Herein, we report a type of GQDs with strong absorption (1070 nm) in NIR-II region that was synthesized via a one-step solvothermal treatment using phenol as single precursor by tuning the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide under a high magnetic field with an intensity of 9T. The obtained 9T-GQDs demonstrate uniform size distribution (3.6 nm), and tunable fluorescence (quantum yield, 16.67%) and high photothermal conversion efficacy (33.45%). In vitro and in vivo results indicate that 9T-GQDs could efficiently ablate tumor cells and inhibit the tumor growth under NIR-II irradiation. Moreover, the 9T-GQDs exhibited enhanced NIR imaging of tumor in living mice, suggesting the great probability of using 9T-GQDs for in vivo NIR imaging-guided PTT in the NIR-II window.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Imaging-guided PTT of 9T-GQDs irradiated under NIR-II laser.

Schematic, morphology, structure, and phase characterization of 9T-GQDs. (a) The schematic representation of the formation of 0T-GQDs and 9T-GQDs, respectively; (b and inset) TEM and HRTEM image of 9T-GQDs. (c and inset) AFM image of 9T-GQDs and the height profile corresponding to the white line shown in (c). (d) XRD diffraction patterns and (e) Raman spectrum of 9T-GQDs.

Surface and optical properties of 9T-GQDs. (a) XPS scanning spectrum of 9T-GQDs. (b and c) XPS high-resolution survey scans of C 1s and O 1s in the 9T-GQDs, respectively. (d and e) Typical EPR and FT-IR spectra of 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs, respectively. (f) UV–vis–NIR absorption and excitation spectra of 9T-GQDs. The inset is the image of 9T-GQDs dispersion in visible and UV light (365 nm).

Photothermal properties and in vitro PTT. (a) Temperatures change of pure water (control) and 9T-GQDs dispersions with different concentrations under NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) for 300 s. (b) Temperature change of 9T-GQDs dispersion (0.2 mg/mL) under NIR-II irradiation time (1064 nm, 1.0 W/cm2); the irradiation was removed after 780 s. (c) Linear time data versus –lnθ obtained from the cooling period shown in Fig. 4b. (d) Cell viability of 4T1 cells treated by 9T-GQDs with and without 5 min exposure to 1.0 W/cm2 NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm). (e–h) Confocal images of 4T1 cells treated by 9T-GQDs at different concentrations (e, 0 μg mL−1; f, 25 μg mL−1; g, 50 μg mL−1; h, 100 μg mL−1) under 5 min NIR-II irradiation, and costained with calcein AM (green, live cells) and propidium iodide (red, dead cells). The scale bar is 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Biosafety and biodistribution of 9T-GQDs. (a) In vitro cytotoxicity of 9T-GQDs at different concentrations on different cell lines including 4T1, Hela and H196. (b) Biodistribution of 9T-GQDs in different organs from 9T-GQDs-treated tumor-bearing mice with different post-treatment time. (c) Ex vivo NIR images of various organs from a mouse treated with 9T-GQDs for 96 h using an excitation wavelength of 710 nm. (d) H&E stained tissue sections of organs from 9T-GQDs (1 mg/mL) and PBS-injected mice. The scale bar represents 100 μm.

Hematological properties of 9T-GQDs. (a–b) the counts of platelet (PLA), serum alanine aminotransferase (SAA), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and white blood cell (WBC) of mice treated by 9T-GQDs or PBS after 24 h post-injection (n = 4). (c) Liver enzyme ratio of ALT to AST from mice treated by PBS (control) and 9T-GQDs for 24 h. Student's two-tailed t-test was used for statistical analysis (n = 4, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01).

Changes in marker genes. Representative RT-PCR expression level from tissue of mice treated by PBS (control) and 9T-GQDs: (a) kidney, (b) liver, (c) lung and (d) spleen, respectively. Student's two-tailed t-test was used for statistical analysis (n = 4, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01).

In vivo PTT of 9T-GQDs. (a) NIR imaging of PBS and 9T-GQDs under an excitation wavelength of 710 nm. (b) bright (left) and NIR imaging (right) of tumor-bearing mouse treated with 9T-GQDs for 2 days. (c) Tumor growth curves of mice received different treatments within 14 days. (d) The body weight curves of mice received different treatments within 14 days. (e) Volume and optical pictures (inset) of tumors obtained from mice on day 14 after receiving different treatments. (f) H&E-stained sections of tumors collected from mice received different treatments at day 14. Student's two-tailed t-test was used for statistical analysis (n = 4, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are considered emerging nanomaterials for photothermal therapy (PTT) of cancer due to their good biocompatibility and rapid excretion. However, the optical absorbance of GQDs in shorter wavelengths (<1000 nm) limits their overall therapeutic efficacies as photothermal agent in the second near infrared window (1000–1700 nm, NIR-II). Herein, we report a type of GQDs with strong absorption (1070 nm) in NIR-II region that was synthesized via a one-step solvothermal treatment using phenol as single precursor by tuning the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide under a high magnetic field with an intensity of 9T. The obtained 9T-GQDs demonstrate uniform size distribution (3.6 nm), and tunable fluorescence (quantum yield, 16.67%) and high photothermal conversion efficacy (33.45%). In vitro and in vivo results indicate that 9T-GQDs could efficiently ablate tumor cells and inhibit the tumor growth under NIR-II irradiation. Moreover, the 9T-GQDs exhibited enhanced NIR imaging of tumor in living mice, suggesting the great probability of using 9T-GQDs for in vivo NIR imaging-guided PTT in the NIR-II window.

ce:section.ce:para: Phototherapy, including photodynamic therapy (PDT) and photothermal therapy (PTT) of cancer has attracted wide attention due to its high precision and spatio-temporal resolution [1–8]. PTT mainly uses nanomaterials to absorb and convert laser energy into local heat under light irradiation, leading to irreversible cell death and tumor destruction [9–12]. Compared with traditional tumor treatment strategies, PTT induced by near-infrared (NIR) light occurs in tumor tissues, with less invasive and side effects, and no obvious damage to normal tissues [13–16]. At present, most nanomaterials with photothermal conversion function exhibit optical absorbance in the first NIR window (700–950 nm, NIR-I) [17–20]. Although great progress has been made in NIR-I-induced PTT, the inevitable absorption and scattering of NIR-I light in organs and tissues seriously reduces the light intensity of NIR laser on tumor sites [21–23]. In order to increase the penetration depth of incident light, cause the deeper tumor injury and improve the therapeutic effect in PTT, it is a better option to apply NIR light to second NIR window (1000–1700 nm, NIR-II) by taking advantage of the high penetration depth of NIR-II light in the body [24–27]. Therefore, it is very important to develop efficient photothermal nanomaterials with high NIR-II absorbance.

In recent years, several research groups have developed NIR-II adsorbed photothermal nanomaterials for tumor PTT irradiated by NIR-II, such as carbon nanotubes, gold nanostructures, copper sulfide, niobium carbide, tin selenide and semiconductor polymers [28–43]. These reported photothermal agents indeed exhibit good therapeutic effects in NIR-II-induced PTT, however, serval drawbacks are closely related to their clinical application including the potential long-term toxicity, nondegradation and poor water-solubility [44,45]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained much attention in biomedical areas due to their simple fabrication, superior water-solubility and chemical stability [46]. The small size distribution of GQDs (<20 nm) enables them to be cleared and excreted by the kidney, thus showing good biocompatibility and non-toxic behavior in the body [47–49]. Combining with their tunable fluorescence emission and unique optical absorbance, these advantages extend the use of GQDs as a potential NIR-responsive photothermal agent for imaging-guided PPT [50–54]. However, the optical absorption of reported GQDs is mainly concentrated in the NIR-I region, which limits their PTT application in the longer wavelength region (>1000 nm). At present, the methods of adjusting the absorbance of GQDs mainly include surface passivation, heteroatom doping and size correction, which cannot achieve NIR-II absorbance of GQDs [55–59]. Therefore, it is of great scientific significance to explore a new strategy for the design of high absorbance GQDs in the NIR-II region.

In this manuscript, we report the successful synthesis of GQDs with strong absorbance (～1070 nm) in NIR-II region and discuss their potential biomedical application for PTT of cancer. The GQDs (named as 9T-GQDs) were prepared via a one-step solvothermal process using phenol molecules as single precursors and hydrogen peroxide as oxidant under an external high magnetic field (HMF) with an intensity of 9 T. The HMF is used to control the concentration of dissolved oxygen in the reaction system and the accordingly formation of superoxide radicals during the decomposition of phenol molecules, leading to the formation of GQDs with abundant CO bonds and larger conjugated system. The as-synthesized 9T-GQDs have abundant hydrophilic groups, good water solubility and small size distribution (3.6 nm). The cytotoxicity and biosafety of 9T-GQDs were tested in vitro and in vivo, indicating their excellent biocompatibility. The 9T-GQDs are used as photothermal agents to kill tumor cells in vitro and inhibit tumor growth in vivo via PTT under NIR-II irradiation respectively (Fig. 1 ). Finally, the 9T-GQDs exhibit tunable fluorescence emission with a great potential for imaging-guided cancer therapy.

All chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), acetone (C3H6O), phenol (C6H6O)， dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were used as received without further purification. The water used in all experiments was of Millipore Milli-Q grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of magnetic-field-induced graphene quantum dots (9T-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Phenol (0.067 g) was dissolved in a solution of 20 mL acetone. After intense sonication for 30 min, 1.0 mL of H2O2 (30%) was slowly added into the solution. The solution was then treated ultrasonically for 10 min and transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave chamber. The precursor solution was heated to and maintained at 200 °C with an external high magnetic field with an intensity of 9T. To observe the influence of high magnetic field on NIR-II absorbance of the as-synthesized GQDs, same experiment has been performed without the introduction of external high magnetic field (0T). After 20 h, the solution was cooled naturally to room temperature. The resultant product was purified with repeated centrifugation at a speed of 12000 rpm for 20 min and redispersion in water for three cycles. Finally, the aqueous dispersion of 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs were dialyzed for 3 days (Spectra/Per molecular porous membrane tubing, cutoff 100, 000) at room temperature. The aqueous dispersion of 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs were then collected and dried to obtain solid 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) in this work was carried out on a Veeco DI Nano-scope multimode V system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution TEM images were measured on a JEOL JEMARM200F TEM/STEM. Powder XRD pattern was acquired from lyophilized samples using D8 Bruker X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The absorption spectra were obtained on a UV–vis–NIR Spectrometer (Lambda 365, PerkinElmer Co., USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by a NICOLET FT-IR spectrometer scanning from 4000 to 400 cm−1 at room temperature. The surface charge was determined using a Zetasizer Nano-ZS at room temperature. The ESR measurement was performed on JES-FA200 with microwave frequency of 8750–9650 MHz (X-band). The photothermal experiments were conducted using a NIR laser with a wavelength of 1064 nm and power density of 1.0 W/cm2 (Lasever Inc, Ningbo, China). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured on an ESCALAB MKII with Al Kα (hυ = 1486.6 eV) as the excitation source. The PL study was carried out on a JOBIN YVON Co. FluoroMax®-3 Spectrofluorometer. Raman spectra were taken on a Raman-Microscope (Renishaw-InVia) using an Ar+ laser with 514.5 nm at room temperature.

Three cell lines including breast cancer cells (4T1), cervical cancer cells (Hela) and lung carcinoma cells (NCI–H196) were seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated overnight in RPMI-1640 media supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. On the following day, the medium was replaced with a medium containing 9T-GQDs or with PBS (control), respectively. 9T-GQDs at different concentrations (25, 50, and 100 μg mL−1) was ran in sextuplicate. Cells were incubated with 9T-GQDs for 72 h. Cells in wells containing the medium without 9T-GQDs were used as the control. Cell viability was assessed by using CellTiter-Glo Luminescent assay (Promega, Madison, WI, USA). Luminescence was measured in a multi-label plate reader (Envision PerkinElmer, USA). The fluorescence intensity from 9T-GQDs treated cells was compared to the intensity from PBS-treated control cells to determine percent viability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Viability of cells treated with 9T-GQDs with or without NIR-II irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated overnight in the aforementioned DMEM. In the following day, the medium was replaced with a medium containing 9T-GQDs or with medium control. Four concentrations of 9T-GQDs including 100 μg mL−1, 50 μg mL−1, 25 μg mL−1 and 12.5 μg mL−1 were used respectively, and samples at each concentration were ran in sextuplicate. The cells were incubated with 9T-GQDs for 72 h. Cells in wells containing the medium without 9T-GQDs were used as the control. For photothermal treatments, the cells were irradiated with 1.0 W/cm2 NIR-II laser for 5 min in the middle of the incubation. Cell viability was assessed using the Alamar Blue assay. Briefly, the medium was replaced with cell culture medium containing the Alamar Blue reagent and incubated for 2 h. Following the incubation, a microplate reader (SpectraMax i3, Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, CA) was used to determine the fluorescence intensity of 9T-GQDs (550ex/590em). The fluorescence intensity from 9T-GQDs in treated cells was compared to the intensity from untreated control cells to determine percent viability.

30 μL 1,3-diphenylisobenzonfuran (DPBF) dispersed in DMSO (10 mmol/L) was added to 9T-GQDs solution. The solution was saturated with Ar atmosphere and kept in the dark and irradiated with a 1064 nm laser (0.4 W/cm2) for 24 min, and the absorption intensity of DPBF at 427 nm was recorded every 2 min. In the control experiments, DPBF plus H2O2 counterparts with 1064 nm irradiation and 9T-GQDs plus H2O2 counterparts without 1064 nm irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal laser scanning microscopic imaging of 9T-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded onto glass cover slips in a 6-well plate. After overnight incubation in water at 37 °C, cells were incubated with 9T-GQDs (0.05 mg/mL) and PBS (control) for 2 h. Cells were then washed with PBS 3 × and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 10 min at 37 °C. Cells were then mounted onto glass slides with ProLong® Gold Antifade Mountant. The images of cells were acquired using a Laser Scanning Microscope Leica SP8X (Leica Microsystems GmbH, Germany). Different excitation wavelengths were used for fluorescent imaging including 405, 488 and 546 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex vivo NIR imaging and biodistribution and blood circulation of 9T-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: All the animal procedures were in agreement with the guidelines for the care and use of laboratory animals of Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China's requirements. Female BALB/c tumor-bearing mice of 5 weeks old were used in this study. 9T-GQDs (200 μL, 1 mg/mL) were injected intravenously into mice. Mice receiving no injection were used as controls. At 24 h, 48 h, 72 h and 96 h after injection, the mice were euthanized and the whole organs of heart, liver, spleen, tumor, lung, and kidneys were harvested. To assess the circulation time of 9T-GQDs (200 μL, 1 mg/mL), blood from 9T-GQDs-treated tumor-bearing mice was collected at different post-injection time including 0.15, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4, 6, and 10 h. The collected blood samples were centrifuged, and the plasma were collected. Plasma from blood collected from PBS-treated mice were used to eliminate background fluorescence. Fluorescence signals of blood and organs from mice were taken by a Xenogen IVIS imaging system at an excitation wavelength of 710 nm.

Four days after receiving intravenous administration of 9T-GQDs at 1 mg/mL, the female BALB/c mice were euthanized and the whole organs (heart, kidney, liver, lung and spleen) were removed and preserved in 10% formalin for 48 h. Tissues were then embedded in paraffin, sliced into 5 μm sections, and stained with hematoxylin and eosin. Microscopic images of tissues were acquired using a Nikon ECLIPSE TE2000-S microscope.

Blood cell panels including white blood cells and platelet and serum alanine aminotransferase and blood urea nitrogen levels were quantified at 24 h after intravenous administration of 200 μL of 1 mg/mL 9T-GQDs in female BALB/c mice (n = 4). Animals receiving PBS injection (n = 4) were used as controls. Blood was drawn from each mouse through cardiac puncture for analysis.

After being intravenously injected for 24 h using 9T-GQDs (1 mg mL−1, dispersed in PBS), 20 mg of each harvested tissue from 9T-GQDs-treated mice were flash-frozen using liquid nitrogen and grounded into fine powder. The powders were then transferred into microfuge tubes and treated with Trizol (Thermo Scientific, Rockford, IL, USA) together with Qiagen RNeasy Mini Kit (Qiagene, Hilden, Germany) for the extraction of total RNA. cDNA was synthesized by Transcriptor First Strand cDNA Synthesis Kit (Roche, Mannheim, Germany). The quantitative real-time PCR was performed in triplicate using a FastStart Essential DNA Green Master (Roche, Mannheim, Germany) on a Roche LightCycler 96 Real Time PCR System. The expression levels of all tested genes were normalized to the expression level of Gapdh. The cDNA was amplified with the following primers:
Target genes forward primer reverse primer
xbp-1s 5′-TGCTGAGTCCGCAGCAGGTG-3′ 5′-GACTAGCAGACTCTGGGGAAGG-3′
chop 5′- GCAGCGACAGAGCCAGAATA-3′ 5′- ACCAGGTTCTGCTTTCAGGT-3′
bip 5′-GTGTGTGAGACCAGAACCGT-3′ 5′-GCAGTCAGGCAGGAGTCTTA-3′
IL-1β 5′- GCCACCTTTTGACAGTGATGAG-3′ 5′- GACAGCCCAGGTCAAAGGTT-3′
TNF-α 5′-ATGGCCTCCCTCTCATCAGT-3′ 5′-TGGTTTGCTACGACGTGGG-3′
Gapdh 5′-GACTTCAACAGCAACTCCCAC-3′ 5′-TCCACCACCCTGTTGCTGTA-3′

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo therapy of 9T-GQDs with or without NIR-II irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: Female BALB/c mice of 5 weeks old were used for in vivo PTT. Mice with tumor were prepared by subcutaneous injection of one million 4T1 cells suspended in Matrigel into the right flanks of these mice. Tumors were allowed to grow for 10 days. Mice bearing tumors were divided into four groups (n = 4 per group). Two groups were intravenously injected with 200 μL of PBS with one if two groups subsequently receiving NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) for 5 min. Another two groups were intravenously injected with 9T-GQDs (200 μL, 1.0 mg/mL) for two times and then one of the two groups were treated with NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) for 5 min. In vivo NIR imaging of 9T-GQDs and NIR-II treated tumor was taken by a Xenogen IVIS imaging system at day 2 using an excitation wavelength of 710 nm and emission wavelength from 695 to 770 nm. Tumor sizes were measured every 2 days for 14 days. The length and width of the tumors were measured with a digital caliper. Tumor volume was calculated by equation V = ab2/2, where a is the length and b is the width of the tumor. Fourteen days after 9T-GQDs injection, tumors were harvested for H&E analysis.

Data were expressed as mean ± SD. The statistical significance of the data was compared by Student's two-tailed t-test. Analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to analyze the differences among the different groups (n = 4) using software Microsoft Excel. The differences were considered to be significant when p < 0.05.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the 9T-GQDs was synthesized via one-step solvothermal treatment using phenol and hydrogen peroxide as precursor under a 9T HMF. The phenol served as carbon precursor and hydrogen peroxide serve as oxidizing agent and acetone serve as organic solvent. The bottom-up formation of 9T-GQDs involves the liquid-gas interfacial growth, which has been proved to be an effective strategy for preparing two-dimensional nanostructures [60]. The whole process should consist of two steps. The one is that phenol molecules tend to form the layered macromolecules through conjugation of electron cloud in the benzene ring under an external HMF (photograph and cartoon of HMF, Fig. S1) [61]. In addition, the decomposed oxygen molecules and gaseous water molecules from hydrogen peroxide at high temperature served as bubble in the acetone solvent. The HMF make for the increase in solubility of oxygen molecules, leading to the production of more bubbles in the reaction system [62]. Then, the layered phenol macromolecules could be adsorbed onto the surface of bubble (oxygen molecule and water molecule) by molecular adsorption or hydrogen bonding. The other one is that the chemical bonds between C–H and benzene ring in macromolecules break at high temperature to form carbon-based radicals, and then form two-dimensional small carbon fragments. The electrons escape from carbon-based free radicals and react with dissolved oxygen molecules and water molecules to generate the free radicals of superoxide anion and hydroxyl radical [63]. Under a HMF, the high active superoxide anions have excellent stability [64,65] and react with two-dimensional small carbon fragments, leading to the formation of functional groups including superoxide bond. The high temperature and pressure caused by solvent gasification and formed oxygen and water molecules further encourage small carbon fragments to form larger functional GQDs due to their supersaturation. In addition, the increased oxygen molecules under external high magnetic field will affect the crystallization of small carbon fragments on the surface of bubble, leading to the formation of more internal vacancy defects.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2b) of 9T-GQDs displays that they are well distributed with an average size distribution of 3.6 nm (Fig. S2). The high-resolution TEM (inset of Fig. 2b) image reveals that the 9T-GQDs are crystal structure with an interplanar distance of about 0.346 nm, which is corresponded to the (002) lattice planes of graphite [66]. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image (Fig. 2c) shows that the 9T-GQDs have a height distribution peak at ~1.7 nm (inset of Fig. 2c), which indicates the 9T-GQDs are composed of two or three layers because the thickness of graphene is about 0.6 nm [67]. To observe the influence of HMF on NIR-II absorbance of the as-synthesized GQDs, the GQDs obtained without HMF (named as 0T-GQDs) have been prepared using same experimental procedure. As shown in Fig. S3 and Fig. S4, the 0T-GQDs demonstrate similar morphology, lattice structure and size distribution and thickness in comparison with 9T-GQDs. The phase composition and surface properties of the 9T-GQDs were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 2d, the characteristic peak at ～22° in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern can be indexed by bulk graphite [68]. Raman spectrum in Fig. 2e shows the high-degree graphitization of 9T-GQDs due to the high intensity ratio (1.21) of G/D band. In addition, abound defects in the 9T-GQDs are confirmed due to the existence of D band at 1366 cm−1 [69]. Similarly, the XRD patterns (Fig. S5) and Raman spectrum (Fig. S6) of 0T-GQDs have demonstrated their high-degree graphitization. Notably, the 9T-GQDs (1.21) manifest the weaker intensity ratio of G/D band than that of 0T-GQDs (1.27). Such the increase in the defect degree has also been confirmed by other GQDs synthesized under different magnetic field intensities including 1.5 T, 3T and 6T (Fig. S6).

The possible growth mechanism and structure difference of GQDs synthesized under different magnetic field have been proposed (Fig. 2a and Fig. S7). When the magnetic field intensity is 0 T, phenol molecules tend to form long-chain intermedium by intermolecular hydrogen bonds. The dissolved oxygen in the solvent provides reaction interface for intermedium adsorption, which further decomposed into carbon-based free radicals and small carbon fragments. Under high temperature and pressure, the carbon fragments grown into functional GQDs due to the existence of hydroxyl radical resulted from the electrons transfer from carbon-based free radicals to water molecules. With the increase in magnetic field intensity (from 1.5 to 3 T), the increased dissolved oxygen provide more reaction interfaces for the decomposition of layered macromolecules, leading to the increase of defect degree in the GQDs. However, the instability of superoxide anion in low magnetic field intensity lead to their difficult reaction with carbon-based fragment. When the magnetic field intensity is 6 T, the superoxide anion becomes more stability, which can react with two-dimensional small carbon fragments and result in the formation of functional GQDs including superoxide bond. In addition, the further increase in dissolved oxygen provide abundant reaction interfaces for the decomposition of layered macromolecules, producing the defect-rich GQDs in the final products.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey of 9T-GQDs in Fig. 3 a shows that carbon (48.74%) and oxygen (51.26%) are present on the surface of 9T-GQDs. The C 1s spectrum in Fig. 3b shows four peaks at 284.5, 284.9, 286.1, and 288.6 eV that come from C–C (sp3)/CC (sp2), C–O (sp3), and O–CO (sp2) [70]. The four peaks in O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) at 532.1, 532.5, 532.9 and 533.9 eV, are assigned to CO quinone-type groups, C–OH phenol groups, C–O–C ether groups and O–O superoxide group [71,72]. The obvious difference between 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs is that the oxygen content (32.36%) of the 0T-GQDs is less than that of 9T-GQDs (Fig. S8). The XPS high-resolution survey scans of O 1s in the 0T-GQDs (Fig. S8c) indicates that they have three peaks at 532.1, 532.5 and 532.9 which belong to CO quinone-type groups, C–OH phenol groups, C–O–C ether groups respectively. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) has widely been used to detect the electronic property of carbon-based nanomaterials with extended π-electron systems. As shown in Fig. 3d, both 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs show a narrow EPR signal at g = 2.0029, which should be from C-related dangling bonds and defects in the GQDs [73,74]. The value of g is similar to that of free electron and is consistent with that of aliphatic carbon-based radicals [75]. However, compared with 0T-GQDs, the 9T-GQDs show another EPR signal at g = 2.0298, which may belong to RO2 · radicals [76].

Fig. 3e shows Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs. The 0T-GQDs have obvious stretching vibration of C–O–C at 1043 cm−1, while the 9T-GQDs exhibit very weaker absorption of C–O–C at same position. Notably, the 9T-GQDs have a strong peak at 1712 cm−1, which is due to the CO bond of carboxylic acid and carbonyl group, while the 0T-GQDs have no obvious absorption of CO bond at same position [77]. Fig. S9 also confirmed that with the increase in intensity of magnetic field, the absorbance peak at ~1712 cm−1 became more apparent, which resulted from that the external HMF induced the chemical reaction between dissolved oxygen radical and small carbon fragments, leading to the formation of CO bond in the final GQDs. The emerging peaks at 1360 and 1170 cm−1 are caused by the vibrations of C–O and O–O, indicating the presence of RO2 · in the 9T-GQDs [78]. The hydrophilic functional groups of 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs located at 3434 cm−1 and 3361 cm−1 endow them with good water-solubility [79]. Above results indicate that the GQDs with functional groups (i.e. CO/COOH and RO2 ·) are obtained by external HMF under the assistant of hydrogen peroxide.

The optical properties of the as-synthesized GQDs were studied. As shown in Fig. 3f and Fig. S10a, both 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs have similar weak UV absorbance, which come from π–π* transition of aromatic domains in the GQDs [80,81]. However, the 9T-GQDs possess two apparent absorbance peaks in 705 nm in NIR-I region and 1070 nm in NIR-II region respectively in comparison with the 0T-GQDs (Fig. S10b). The strong NIR-II absorbance of 9T-GQDs should attribute to the larger conjugated system (π–π* transition and n–π* transition from abundant CO bonds) containing plentiful delocalized π electrons in the layered 9T-GQDs [50,82]. The excitation spectrum of the 9T-GQDs (Fig. 3f) gained at the emission wavelength at 380 nm has an obvious peak at 369 nm. When the 9T-GQDs solution was exposed to UV light at the wavelength of 365 nm, blue emission was obviously observed (see inset in Fig. 3f).

Fig. 4 a and Fig. S11 show the photothermal conversion effect of 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs under NIR-II irradiation (1064 nm, 1.0 W/cm2). Results show that the temperature changes are proportional to the concentration of 9T-GQDs. However, the temperatures of water (control) and 0T-GQDs have no obvious change under the same irradiation condition and solution concentration, which indicated that the 9T-GQDs have a promising candidate for PTT under NIR-II irradiation. According to the heat transfer time constant from Fig. 4b and the reported calculation method of photothermal conversion efficiency (PCE) (Fig. 4c), the PCE of 9T-GQDs can be as high as 33.45%, which is similar to the efficiency exhibited by conjugated polymer nanoparticles (30.1%) and Au–Cu9S5 nanoparticles (37%) and lower than the ultrathin polypyrrole nanosheets (64.6%) and semiconducting polymer-based nanoparticles (66.4%) [28,29,38,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Viability of cells treated with 9T-GQDs with or without NIR-II irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cell line was selected as model to conduct the in vitro PTT of 9T-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4d, more than 96% of cells survived after being treated with 9T-GQDs for 72 h without NIR-II irradiation, which indicates that 9T-GQDs are nontoxic to the tested cells at concentrations up to 100 μg mL−1. However, NIR-II irradiation produced an enormous reduce (down to 43%) in cell viability when 4T1 cells were treated with 100 μg mL−1 of 9T-GQDs. Conversely, alone NIR-II irradiation (cells not incubated with 9T-GQDs) had a negligible effect on their cell viability (Fig. S12). Calcein AM (hydrolyzes to green-fluorescent calcein in live cells) and PI (stains DNA of dead cells red) co-staining was used to differentiate live and dead cells after being treated with 9T-GQDs at different concentration under NIR-II irradiation. In the control group, only the green fluorescence signal was observed, indicating that cells remained alive after subjected to the irradiation (Fig. 4e). With the increase in concentration of 9T-GQDs (Fig. 4f–h), more dead cells were observed after exposure to NIR-II irradiation. These results indicate that alone NIR-II irradiation at the tested power had no ability to kill cancer cells, while NIR irradiation plus 9T-GQDs treatment led to effective cell death. Meanwhile, the main phototoxic mechanism of the 9T-GQDs should come from their photothermal effect because unconspicuous toxic reactive oxygen species (ROS) was produced by 9T-GQDs under 1064 nm irradiation for 5 min (Fig. S13). Such conclusion had been confirmed by the singlet oxygen quantum yield of 9T-GQDs at 1064 nm (0.22%) using indocyanine green (ICG) as a standard (Fig. S14).

The detailed photoluminescence (PL) studies of 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs were performed under different excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. S15, when the excitation wavelength increases from 260 nm to 500 nm, the emission peak red-shifts to longer wavelength and the PL intensity firstly increases and then decreases. The maximum PL emissions of 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs locate at 481 nm and 485 nm respectively at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. In addition, the 9T-GQDs have demonstrated upconverted PL emission (Fig. S16) excited by long-wavelength light (from 700 nm to 960 nm). The PL quantum yield of the 9T-GQDs and 0T-GQDs were determined to be 16.67% and 9.45% respectively, using rhodamine B as a standard. The increase in quantum yield of 9T-GQDs should be attributable to their abundant conjugated π-electrons in sp2 network compared to 0T-GQDs [50,83]. The 9T-GQDs were used to quantify their PL stability over time. As shown in Fig. S17, no obvious change in PL intensity was observed after 60 min exposure under an excitation wavelength of ƛex = 380 nm.

Considering that 9T-GQDs have tunable fluorescence emission and good PL stability, the imaging capability of 9T-GQDs was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S18, 4T1 cells treated by 9T-GQDs could generate an apparent fluorescence signal. At different excitation wavelengths including 405, 488, and 546 nm, the confocal images of 4T1 cells treated by 9T-GQDs presented blue, green and red respectively. The confocal images (Fig. S19) of 4T1 cells treated with PBS (control) have no fluorescence emission, suggesting that the multicolor signals should be produced by 9T-GQDs. In addition, 9T-GQDs-treated 4T1 cells showed no significant decrease in fluorescence signal after continuous irradiation for 60 min (Fig. S20), suggesting that 9T-GQDs can be used for long-term cell imaging. Notably, 9T-GQDs could across cytomembrane and exist in the cytoplasm of 4T1 cells, indicating their potential as therapeutic agent for cancer therapy.

The biosafety of 9T-GQDs in vitro and in vivo was further evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5 a, more than 91.7% of cells from three tumor cell lines (4T1, Hela and H196) survived after 72 h post-treatment with a concentration of 9T-GQDs up to 500 μg mL−1, indicating the no cytotoxicity of 9T-GQDs. Fig. 5b shows the biodistribution of 9T-GQDs in vivo. Tumor-bearing mice were treated by 9T-GQDs (dose = 200 μL, 1 mg/mL) and PBS (volume = 200 μL) via an intravenous injection respectively. After being sacrificed, the organs in mice containing liver, spleen, kidney, tumor, lung and heart were collected at different post-injection time. The biodistribution of 9T-GQDs was measured by NIR fluorescence signal of 9T-GQDs (Fig. S21). Results shown that 9T-GQDs mainly existed in the liver of mice after 24 h post-treatment, which was updated by the reticuloendothelial system. The signal of 9T-GQDs was also found in the kidney, spleen, lung, tumor and heart. After 96 h injection (Fig. 5c), the liver, kidney, spleen and tumor still showed significant NIR fluorescence signals, and about 12% of 9T-GQDs were accumulated in the liver. Histological analysis was performed on the main organs of 9T-GQDs and PBS treated mice to evaluate the biocompatibility of 9T-GQDs. The half-life in blood is 1.59 h (Fig. S22), indicating that the 9T-GQDs are eliminated from the body by metabolism. The images in Fig. 5d shown that the organs treated by 9T-GQDs and PBS have similar cell morphology, indicating the excellent biocompatibility of 9T-GQDs.

Considering the larger accumulation in liver and spleen, the biosafety of 9T-GQDs in liver and kidney was further analyzed by monitoring their serum markers in the blood. Fig. 6 a and b demonstrated the levels of corresponding serum markers from 9T-GQDs and PBS-treated mice (control), respectively. Results shown that the counts of platelet (PLA), serum alanine aminotransferase (SAA), blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and white blood cell (WBC) in 9T-GQDs-treated mice were similar to that in PBS-treated mice. Aspartate transaminase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is two kinds of enzymes associated with hepatoxicity. No obvious difference in ALT-to-AST ratio was observed in PBS and 9T-GQDs treated mice (Fig. 6c). Above results show that 9T-GQDs had no obvious toxicity to liver and kidney although most 9T-GQDs accumulated in liver and kidney.

Quantitative reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) was used to detect alterations in transcription levels for genes involved with the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress response (bip, chop, IL-b, TNF-a, and xbp). Fig. 7 compare mRNA expression in the kidneys, liver, lung, and spleen between 9T-GQDs and PBS-treated mice for 24 h 9T-GQDs treated groups was compared with normalized gene expression levels from mice treated with PBS (control). Gene expression levels were observed to be normal in mice treated with 9TGQDs in the kidneys, liver, lung and spleen, indicating that the 9T-GQDs is no toxicity at the genetic level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging-guided PTT in the NIR-II window
ce:section.ce:para: Given the excellent photothermal properties and biosafety, NIR-II induced PTT was carried out using 9T-GQDs as photothermal agent. Tumor-bearing mice with 10 days grown were randomly divided into 4 groups and each group has 4 mice. The two groups were treated with PBS solution and the other two groups were treated with 9T-GQDs respectively. After being intravenously injected for 24 h, the tumors were irradiated or not irradiated with NIR-II laser (1064 nm, 1.0 W/cm2) for 5 min. Tumor sizes in these groups were collected every 2 days until 14 days after treatment. As shown in Fig. 8 a and b, the bright NIR fluorescence generated by 9T-GQDs after two days post-injection can illuminate the tumor site of the treated mice under the irradiation of 710 nm NIR laser. The accumulation of 9T-GQDs at tumors site can be attributed to two factors. The one is the enhanced permeability and retention effect of nanoparticle [84]. The other one is that the nanoparticles-induced endothelial leakiness known as “NanoEL”. In this case, the homophilic interaction of vascular endothelial cadherin between endothelial cell may be disrupted by 9T-GQDs, which forms a micrometer-sized gaps in blood vessel walls and lead to the entry of 9T-GQDs to tumor microenvironment [85–90]. This bright NIR fluorescence from tumor sites in mice treated with 9T-GQDsprovides great potential for imaging-guided PTT.

Fig. 8c demonstrates the rapid tumor growth in both PBS-injected groups even after being irradiated with NIR-II light. Tumor growth in mice treated by 9T-GQDs without NIR-II irradiation was similar to that of mice treated with PBS. For all three groups, the tumors grown increased rapidly by～6.2 times in comparison with the initial tumor size. On the contrary, the tumor growth was significantly inhibited in mice treated with 9T-GQDs under NIR-II irradiation. Fig. 8d manifest the average weight for all four groups of mice over 14 days. The body weight of mice in each group was maintained within the normal range, and no significant weight change was observed. After 14 days treatment, all mouse tumors were collected and the volume and weight were measured respectively. The mean tumor volume (Fig. 8e) and weight (Fig. S23) from NIR-II irradiated and 9T-GQDs-treated group was observably lower than that of the other three groups. To further illustrate the effects of PTT on tumor sites, tumor tissues were collected in each group after 14 days treatment. Histological images of H&E stained tumor sections (Fig. 8f) showed that the tumor cells in PBS, PBS + NIR-II treated group and 9T-GQDs treated group basically retained normal cancerous morphology, with obvious membrane and nuclear structure. In contrast, the tumor cells in the 9T-GQDs + NIR-II treated group were badly damaged, presenting with atrophy, loss of contact, and nuclear damage. The above results indicate that 9T-GQDs can be used as an effective phototherapeutic agent for cancer therapy under NIR-II irradiation.

In summary, we have successfully demonstrated a simple bottom-up strategy for synthesizing metal-free GQDs with strong optical absorbance in the NIR-II region. The external HMF can improve the optical absorbance and quantum yield of the as-synthesized GQDs by tuning the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide to from superoxide radicals, leading to the formation of larger conjugated system in the GQDs. The as-synthesized GQDs manifest a uniform morphology with smaller size distribution and good water-solubility and excellent biocompatibility. Both in vitro and in vivo results show that the as-synthesized GQDs could efficiently kill tumor cells and inhibit the tumor grown at a 1064 nm laser with a power density of 1.0 W/cm2. The as-synthesized 9T-GQDs also exhibit obviously NIR imaging of tumor site in living mice, suggesting the great probability of 9T-GQDs for NIR imaging-guided PTT.
